assessment_items.question_content:
**Originally represented in paint, what object did Hegeso once hold in her hand? **

![Grave stele of Hegeso, c. 410 B.C.E., marble and paint, from the Dipylon Cemetery, Athens, 5 feet 2 inches high (National Archaeological Museum, Athens)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a87fb9d154e9fb36268229044d0e66c2.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The stele shows Hegeso seated before an open jewelry box held by her servant.

Hegeso was originally shown holding a necklace, rendered in paint that is no longer seen.    

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/classical/v/hegeso-stele)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**In addition to providing Hegeso's name, what other information is included in the inscription on this stele?  **

![Grave stele of Hegeso, c. 410 B.C.E., marble and paint, from the Dipylon Cemetery, Athens, 5 feet 2 inches high (National Archaeological Museum, Athens)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a87fb9d154e9fb36268229044d0e66c2.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Women's social standing in ancient Greece relied on their relationships to men.

The inscription includes the name of Hegeso's father, identifying her as the daughter of Proxenos.


**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/classical/v/hegeso-stele)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which sculpture is most similar in function to the *Grave Stele of Hegeso*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Like the *Grave Stele of Hegeso*, kouros and kore figures functioned as grave markers in the Archaic period.  

The practice of wealthy families to commission private funerary sculpture fell out of favor in the Classical period, when more public works were produced.  The *Grave Stele of Hegeso* reflects a resurgence of this tradition in the late 5th century BCE.  

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/classical/v/hegeso-stele)**

choices.content: ![Marble Statue of a kouros (New York Kouros), c. 600–580 B.C.E. (Attic, archaic period), Naxian marble, 194.6 x 51.6 cm (The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ede774d2971d3f587d4c4ee2241a98fc.jpg)

![Polykleitos, Doryphoros (Spear-Bearer), Roman marble copy after a Greek bronze original from c. 450–440 B.C.E. (Museo Archaeologico Nazionale, Naples)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c09117f15dfdfedeed9a356f47751eb8.jpg)

![Phidias (?), Sacrificial Procession (block VIII of the east frieze of the Parthenon), 445–438 B.C.E., marble (The British Museum, London)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5b3b0556a4a3f35b02757cbd0e3e95d3.jpg)

![Nike Adjusting Her Sandal, from the south side of the parapet of the Temple of Athena Nike, Acropolis, Athens, Greece, c. 410 B.C.E., marble, 3 feet 6 inches high (Acropolis Museum, Athens)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/56c5b3cfa2c48499a5276d21b9ac9b3b.jpg)

assessment_items.question_content:
**What do the architectural details surrounding Hegeso and her servant reveal about ancient Greek culture?**
![Grave stele of Hegeso, c. 410 B.C.E., marble and paint, from the Dipylon Cemetery, Athens, 5 feet 2 inches high (National Archaeological Museum, Athens)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a87fb9d154e9fb36268229044d0e66c2.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The pilasters and pediment frame the figures to show they occupy an domestic interior space.

Women in ancient Greece were restricted to the domestic sphere.  They did not have the right to vote and their social standing was defined by their relationships to men.  

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/classical/v/hegeso-stele)**

choices.content: that women occupied a domestic sphere
that wealthy families had private temples
that mortals were considered gods after death
that building techniques changed in the late 5th century

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which detail best reveals a sense of body mass in the shallow space of this relief?**

![Grave stele of Hegeso, c. 410 B.C.E., marble and paint, from the Dipylon Cemetery, Athens, 5 feet 2 inches high (National Archaeological Museum, Athens)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a87fb9d154e9fb36268229044d0e66c2.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The artists shows the drapery around Hegeso's legs fall behind the chair while the drapery at her waist falls in front, thus suggesting the full width of the figure in the shallow relief.   

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/classical/v/hegeso-stele)**

choices.content: the foot does not touch the ground
the drapery falls on both sides of the chair
the detailed curvature of the chair
the smaller scale of the servant

assessment_items.question_content:
**The *Grave Stele of Hegeso* is similar to this sculpture from the Parthenon in all EXCEPT**

![Grave stele of Hegeso, c. 410 B.C.E., marble and paint, from the Dipylon Cemetery, Athens, 5 feet 2 inches high (National Archaeological Museum, Athens)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dc759eabf471e715e6f6ae4ae1771218.jpg) 

![Gods and goddesses of Mount Olympus (detail), Phidias(?), frieze, Parthenon, c. 447–432 B.C.E. (The British Museum, London)](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/43e1141e5966dda4e01d431fc96f5139.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The *Grave Stele of Hegeso* shares this sculpture's Classical characteristics in its drapery treatment, spatial illusionism, and an implied narrative that connects the figures.

Unlike the Parthenon frieze which was a public commission, the **Grave Stele of Hegeso was created for a private patron.  

**[Watch this video to review.](/humanities/ancient-art-civilizations/greek-art/classical/v/hegeso-stele)**

choices.content: the use of narrative subject matter.
